11.2 Few-Shot and Zero-Shot Learning . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 16 11.3 Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 17 11.4 Diffusion Models . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 17 11.5 Multimodal Models . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 17 11.6 Mixture of Experts (MoE) . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 17 12 Summary Table of Key Terms 18 Appendix: Sample RAG Test Questions 18 2 1 Introduction This document serves as a structured reference corpus for evaluating Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipelines. It contains definitions, explanations, comparisons, and fac- tual claims across multiple domains of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML). Each section is deliberately self-contained, with explicit definitions and named concepts, enabling precise retrieval and answer verification. The document is organized into the following major areas:

concepts, enabling precise retrieval and answer verification. The document is organized into the following major areas: 1. Foundations of Artificial Intelligence 2. Core Machine Learning Concepts 3. Deep Learning and Neural Networks 4. Natural Language Processing 5. Reinforcement Learning 6. Model Evaluation and Metrics 7. Data Engineering for ML 8. Ethics and Responsible AI 9. Optimization and Training Techniques 10. Emerging Paradigms 2 Foundations of Artificial Intelligence 2.1 Definition of Artificial Intelligence Artificial Intelligence (AI)is the field of computer science dedicated to creating sys- tems capable of performing tasks that would typically require human intelligence. These tasks include reasoning, learning, problem-solving, perception, language understanding, and decision-making. AI was formally founded as a discipline at theDartmouth Conferencein 1956, organized by John McCarthy, Marvin Minsky, Nathaniel Rochester, and Claude Shannon. John

and decision-making. AI was formally founded as a discipline at theDartmouth Conferencein 1956, organized by John McCarthy, Marvin Minsky, Nathaniel Rochester, and Claude Shannon. John McCarthy is widely credited with coining the term “Artificial Intelligence.” 2.2 Symbolic AI Symbolic AI, also known asGood Old-Fashioned AI (GOFAI), is an approach to AI that represents knowledge explicitly using symbols and logical rules. Programs in Symbolic AI manipulate these symbols according to predefined rules to draw conclusions. A key example of Symbolic AI is theExpert System, which encodes domain knowledge asif-thenrules and uses an inference engine to derive answers. Expert systems were widely adopted in the 1980s for tasks such as medical diagnosis (e.g., MYCIN) and financial planning. Limitations of Symbolic AI: 3 •Brittle: fails when inputs deviate from predefined rules. •Expensive to maintain as knowledge bases grow. •Cannot learn from data or generalize beyond explicit rules.

Limitations of Symbolic AI: 3 •Brittle: fails when inputs deviate from predefined rules. •Expensive to maintain as knowledge bases grow. •Cannot learn from data or generalize beyond explicit rules. 2.3 Sub-symbolic AI Sub-symbolic AIrefers to approaches where knowledge is represented implicitly through numerical parameters, such as weights in a neural network. Unlike Symbolic AI, sub- symbolic systems learn representations directly from data without requiring explicit rule encoding. 2.4 The Turing Test TheTuring Test, proposed by Alan Turing in his 1950 paper“Computing Machinery and Intelligence”, is a behavioral test of machine intelligence. In the test, a human evaluator engages in natural language conversations with both a human and a machine. If the evaluator cannot reliably distinguish the machine from the human, the machine is said to have passed the test. The Turing Test has been criticized for testingbehavioral mimicryrather than genuine understanding or consciousness.

said to have passed the test. The Turing Test has been criticized for testingbehavioral mimicryrather than genuine understanding or consciousness. 2.5 Strong AI vs. Weak AI •Weak AI (Narrow AI):AI systems designed and trained for a specific task. Examples include image classifiers, recommendation engines, and speech recognition systems. All current AI systems fall into this category. •Strong AI (Artificial General Intelligence, AGI):A hypothetical AI system with the capacity to understand, learn, and apply knowledge across any intellectual task as competently as a human being. No AGI system currently exists. 3 Core Machine Learning Concepts 3.1 Definition of Machine Learning Machine Learning (ML)is a subfield of AI in which systems learn from data to improve their performance on a task without being explicitly programmed. The term was coined byArthur Samuelin 1959, who defined it as:“the field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed.”

byArthur Samuelin 1959, who defined it as:“the field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed.” A formal definition byTom Mitchell(1997) states: A computer program is said to learn fromexperience Ewith respect to sometask Tandperformance measure P, if its per- formance at T, as measured by P, improves with experience E. 4 3.2 Types of Machine Learning 3.2.1 Supervised Learning Supervised Learningis a type of ML where the model is trained on a labeled dataset — that is, each training example consists of an input-output pair (x i, yi). The model learns a mapping functionf:X→Ythat can predict outputs for unseen inputs. Examples: •Classification:Predicting a discrete label (e.g., spam vs. not spam). •Regression:Predicting a continuous value (e.g., house prices). Common Algorithms:Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, Sup- port Vector Machines (SVM), k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN), Random Forests, Gradient Boosting.

Common Algorithms:Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, Sup- port Vector Machines (SVM), k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN), Random Forests, Gradient Boosting. 3.2.2 Unsupervised Learning Unsupervised Learningis a type of ML where the model is trained on unlabeled data and must discover hidden structure or patterns on its own. There are no target labels provided. Examples: •Clustering:Grouping similar data points (e.g., k-Means, DBSCAN). •Dimensionality Reduction:Compressing data while preserving structure (e.g., PCA, t-SNE, UMAP). •Generative Modeling:Learning the data distribution to generate new samples. 3.2.3 Semi-supervised Learning Semi-supervised Learningcombines a small amount of labeled data with a large amount of unlabeled data during training. This approach is practical when labeling data is expensive or time-consuming. 3.2.4 Self-supervised Learning Self-supervised Learningis a form of unsupervised learning where the model generates

is expensive or time-consuming. 3.2.4 Self-supervised Learning Self-supervised Learningis a form of unsupervised learning where the model generates its own supervisory signal from unlabeled data. For example, a language model trained to predict the next word in a sentence uses self-supervision. Large language models like GPT are trained via self-supervised learning. 3.2.5 Reinforcement Learning Reinforcement Learning (RL)is a type of ML where anagentlearns to make decisions by interacting with anenvironment. The agent receivesrewardsorpenaltiesbased on its actions and aims to maximize cumulative reward over time. RL is covered in detail in Section 6. 5 3.3 The Bias-Variance Tradeoff TheBias-Variance Tradeoffis a fundamental concept describing the tension between two sources of error in a learning model: •Bias:Error introduced by incorrect assumptions in the learning algorithm. High bias causesunderfitting— the model is too simple to capture the true relationship in the data.

•Bias:Error introduced by incorrect assumptions in the learning algorithm. High bias causesunderfitting— the model is too simple to capture the true relationship in the data. •Variance:Error from sensitivity to small fluctuations in the training data. High variance causesoverfitting— the model memorizes noise in training data and fails to generalize. The total expected error of a model can be decomposed as: Expected Error = Bias 2 + Variance + Irreducible Noise Irreducible noise is inherent in the data and cannot be eliminated. 3.4 Overfitting and Underfitting Overfittingoccurs when a model learns the training data too well, including its noise, resulting in poor generalization to new data. Underfittingoccurs when a model is too simple to capture the underlying pattern in the data, resulting in poor performance on both training and test sets. Mitigation strategies for overfitting: •Regularization (L1/L2) •Dropout (in neural networks) •Cross-validation •Pruning (in decision trees)

Mitigation strategies for overfitting: •Regularization (L1/L2) •Dropout (in neural networks) •Cross-validation •Pruning (in decision trees) •Reducing model complexity •Early stopping during training 3.5 Cross-Validation Cross-Validationis a model evaluation technique that partitions data into multiple subsets to assess how well a model generalizes to an independent dataset. The most common form isk-Fold Cross-Validation, where the data is split intokequally sized folds. The model is trained onk−1 folds and evaluated on the remaining fold, repeated ktimes. Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV)is a special case wherekequals the number of data points. 6 4 Deep Learning and Neural Networks 4.1 Definition of Deep Learning Deep Learningis a subfield of ML based onartificial neural networkswith multiple layers (hence “deep”). These layers learn hierarchical representations of data, with each layer extracting increasingly abstract features.

layers (hence “deep”). These layers learn hierarchical representations of data, with each layer extracting increasingly abstract features. Deep learning has achieved state-of-the-art results in image recognition, speech synthesis, machine translation, and game playing. 4.2 The Artificial Neuron (Perceptron) The basic computational unit of a neural network is theartificial neuron(orperceptron). It computes a weighted sum of its inputs, adds a bias, and passes the result through an activation function: y=f nX i=1 wixi +b ! wherew i are weights,x i are inputs,bis the bias, andfis the activation function. 4.3 Activation Functions Activation functionsintroduce non-linearity into neural networks, enabling them to learn complex patterns. •Sigmoid:σ(x) = 1 1+e−x . Outputs in range (0,1). Prone to vanishing gradients. •Tanh:tanh(x) = ex−e−x ex+e−x . Outputs in range (−1,1). •ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit):f(x) = max(0, x). Most commonly used; avoids vanishing gradient for positive inputs.

•Tanh:tanh(x) = ex−e−x ex+e−x . Outputs in range (−1,1). •ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit):f(x) = max(0, x). Most commonly used; avoids vanishing gradient for positive inputs. •Leaky ReLU:f(x) = max(αx, x) for smallα >0. Avoids dead neurons. •Softmax:Converts a vector of logits into a probability distribution. Used in the output layer of multi-class classifiers. •GELU (Gaussian Error Linear Unit):Used in transformers. Smoother ap- proximation to ReLU. 4.4 Backpropagation Backpropagationis the algorithm used to compute gradients of the loss function with respect to each weight in the network. It applies thechain ruleof calculus, propagating the error signal backward from the output layer to the input layer. Steps: 1. Forward pass: compute predictions and loss. 2. Backward pass: compute gradients of the loss with respect to each weight. 3. Update weights using an optimization algorithm (e.g., gradient descent). 7 4.5 Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)

2. Backward pass: compute gradients of the loss with respect to each weight. 3. Update weights using an optimization algorithm (e.g., gradient descent). 7 4.5 Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) AConvolutional Neural Network (CNN)is a type of neural network designed for processing grid-like data such as images. CNNs useconvolutional layersthat apply learnable filters across the input, enabling the network to detect local features such as edges, textures, and shapes. Key components: •Convolutional Layer:Applies a set of learnable filters to the input using convo- lution operations. •Pooling Layer:Reduces spatial dimensions (e.g., max pooling, average pooling). •Fully Connected Layer:Connects all neurons to produce final predictions. Notable CNN architectures:LeNet-5 (1998), AlexNet (2012), VGGNet (2014), ResNet (2015), EfficientNet (2019). 4.6 Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) ARecurrent Neural Network (RNN)is a type of neural network designed for se-

(2015), EfficientNet (2019). 4.6 Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) ARecurrent Neural Network (RNN)is a type of neural network designed for se- quential data. Unlike feedforward networks, RNNs have connections that form directed cycles, allowing information to persist over time steps. Limitations of vanilla RNNs: •Vanishing gradient problem:Gradients diminish as they are propagated back through many time steps, preventing learning of long-range dependencies. •Exploding gradient problem:Gradients grow exponentially. 4.7 Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)is a type of RNN architecture introduced by Hochreiter and Schmidhuber in 1997, designed to address the vanishing gradient problem. LSTMs use gating mechanisms to control the flow of information: •Forget Gate:Decides what information to discard from cell state. •Input Gate:Decides what new information to store in cell state. •Output Gate:Decides what part of cell state to output. 4.8 The Transformer Architecture

•Input Gate:Decides what new information to store in cell state. •Output Gate:Decides what part of cell state to output. 4.8 The Transformer Architecture TheTransformerarchitecture was introduced in the landmark 2017 paper“Attention Is All You Need”by Vaswani et al. It relies entirely on a mechanism calledself-attention to model relationships between all positions in a sequence, without recurrence. Core components: •Multi-Head Self-Attention:Allows the model to attend to different parts of the input simultaneously. •Positional Encoding:Injects information about token positions into the model. 8 •Feed-Forward Sublayer:Applied independently to each position. •Layer Normalization and Residual Connections:Stabilize training. The Transformer has become the dominant architecture for NLP and is increasingly used in vision (Vision Transformer, ViT) and multimodal systems. 5 Natural Language Processing 5.1 Definition of NLP

The Transformer has become the dominant architecture for NLP and is increasingly used in vision (Vision Transformer, ViT) and multimodal systems. 5 Natural Language Processing 5.1 Definition of NLP Natural Language Processing (NLP)is a subfield of AI and linguistics focused on enabling computers to understand, interpret, and generate human language. NLP combines techniques from computer science, linguistics, and machine learning. 5.2 Key NLP Tasks •Tokenization:Splitting text into units called tokens (words, subwords, or charac- ters). •Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging:Labeling each word with its grammatical role (noun, verb, adjective, etc.). •Named Entity Recognition (NER):Identifying and classifying named entities (people, places, organizations) in text. •Sentiment Analysis:Determining the emotional tone of a text (positive, negative, neutral). •Machine Translation:Automatically translating text from one language to an- other.

•Sentiment Analysis:Determining the emotional tone of a text (positive, negative, neutral). •Machine Translation:Automatically translating text from one language to an- other. •Question Answering (QA):Extracting or generating answers to questions from a text corpus. •Text Summarization:Producing a shorter version of a text while preserving key information. •Coreference Resolution:Identifying when different words refer to the same en- tity. 5.3 Word Embeddings Word Embeddingsare dense vector representations of words in a continuous vector space, where semantically similar words are mapped to nearby points. Key models: •Word2Vec(Mikolov et al., 2013): Trains shallow neural networks to predict con- text words (Skip-gram) or predict a word from its context (CBOW). •GloVe(Pennington et al., 2014): Learns embeddings from global word co-occurrence statistics. •FastText(Bojanowski et al., 2017): Extends Word2Vec by representing words as

•GloVe(Pennington et al., 2014): Learns embeddings from global word co-occurrence statistics. •FastText(Bojanowski et al., 2017): Extends Word2Vec by representing words as bags of character n-grams, handling out-of-vocabulary words. 9 5.4 Large Language Models (LLMs) Large Language Models (LLMs)are transformer-based models trained on massive text corpora using self-supervised objectives. They demonstrate strong performance across a wide range of NLP tasks viain-context learningandprompt engineering. Notable LLMs: •GPT-3(OpenAI, 2020): 175 billion parameters; trained on 570 GB of text. •BERT(Google, 2018): Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers; pre-trained on masked language modeling and next sentence prediction. •T5(Google, 2020): Treats every NLP task as text-to-text. •LLaMA(Meta AI, 2023): Open-weight LLMs available in 7B–65B parameter sizes. •Claude(Anthropic): Constitutional AI-trained LLM family designed with safety as a core principle.

•LLaMA(Meta AI, 2023): Open-weight LLMs available in 7B–65B parameter sizes. •Claude(Anthropic): Constitutional AI-trained LLM family designed with safety as a core principle. •Gemini(Google DeepMind): Multimodal LLM family supporting text, image, audio, and video. 5.5 Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)is a technique that combines a retrieval system with a generative language model. Given a query, a retriever fetches relevant documents from a knowledge base, which are then provided as context to the generator to produce a grounded response. Components of a RAG system: 1.Document Store:A corpus of text chunks or documents. 2.Embedding Model:Encodes queries and documents into vector representations. 3.Vector Database:Stores document embeddings for fast approximate nearest- neighbor search (e.g., FAISS, Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma). 4.Retriever:Selects top-krelevant document chunks based on embedding similarity.

neighbor search (e.g., FAISS, Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma). 4.Retriever:Selects top-krelevant document chunks based on embedding similarity. 5.Generator:An LLM that synthesizes a response conditioned on the retrieved context. RAG reduces hallucination by grounding the model’s responses in retrieved factual con- tent. 6 Reinforcement Learning 6.1 Definition Reinforcement Learning (RL)is a paradigm where an agent learns optimal behavior by interacting with an environment through trial and error. The agent takes actions in states, receives scalar reward signals, and aims to maximize the cumulative discounted return. 10 6.2 Key Components of RL •Agent:The learner and decision-maker. •Environment:The system the agent interacts with. •State (s):A representation of the current situation. •Action (a):A choice made by the agent. •Reward (r):A scalar feedback signal received after taking an action. •Policy (π):A mapping from states to actions.

•Action (a):A choice made by the agent. •Reward (r):A scalar feedback signal received after taking an action. •Policy (π):A mapping from states to actions. •Value Function (V π(s)):Expected cumulative reward from statesunder policy π. •Q-Function (Q π(s, a)):Expected cumulative reward from taking actionain state sunder policyπ. 6.3 The Markov Decision Process (MDP) RL problems are formally modeled as aMarkov Decision Process (MDP), defined by the tuple (S, A, P, R, γ): •S: Set of states •A: Set of actions •P(s ′|s, a): Transition probability •R(s, a): Reward function •γ∈[0,1): Discount factor TheMarkov Propertystates that the future depends only on the current state, not on the history:P(s t+1|st, at) =P(s t+1|s0, . . . , st, at). 6.4 Key RL Algorithms •Q-Learning:A model-free, off-policy algorithm that learns the Q-function directly. Introduced by Watkins (1989). •SARSA:An on-policy TD control algorithm. •Deep Q-Network (DQN):Combines Q-learning with deep neural networks. Used

Introduced by Watkins (1989). •SARSA:An on-policy TD control algorithm. •Deep Q-Network (DQN):Combines Q-learning with deep neural networks. Used by DeepMind to achieve superhuman performance on Atari games (2013). •Policy Gradient Methods:Directly optimize the policy using gradient ascent on expected return (e.g., REINFORCE algorithm). •Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO):A popular policy gradient method used in training LLMs via RLHF. •Actor-Critic:Combines policy gradient (actor) with a value function estimator (critic). 11 6.5 Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) RLHFis a technique used to align language models with human preferences. The process involves: 1. Fine-tuning a pre-trained LLM using supervised learning on demonstration data. 2. Training areward modelon human preference data (comparisons between model outputs). 3. Fine-tuning the LLM using RL (typically PPO) to maximize the reward model’s score.

2. Training areward modelon human preference data (comparisons between model outputs). 3. Fine-tuning the LLM using RL (typically PPO) to maximize the reward model’s score. RLHF was central to training ChatGPT (OpenAI) and is widely adopted in modern LLM alignment workflows. 7 Model Evaluation and Metrics 7.1 Classification Metrics Confusion Matrix:A table summarizing the predictions of a classifier against actual labels: •True Positive (TP):Correctly predicted positive. •True Negative (TN):Correctly predicted negative. •False Positive (FP):Incorrectly predicted positive (Type I error). •False Negative (FN):Incorrectly predicted negative (Type II error). Derived metrics: •Accuracy: T P+T N T P+T N+F P+F N •Precision: T P T P+F P — of all predicted positives, how many are correct? •Recall (Sensitivity): T P T P+F N — of all actual positives, how many were found? •F1 Score: 2·Precision·Recall Precision+Recall — harmonic mean of precision and recall.

•Recall (Sensitivity): T P T P+F N — of all actual positives, how many were found? •F1 Score: 2·Precision·Recall Precision+Recall — harmonic mean of precision and recall. •AUC-ROC:Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve. Measures discriminative ability; 1.0 is perfect, 0.5 is random. 7.2 Regression Metrics •Mean Absolute Error (MAE): 1 n P |yi −ˆyi| •Mean Squared Error (MSE): 1 n P(yi −ˆyi)2 •Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE): √ MSE •R-squared (R 2):Proportion of variance in the target explained by the model. Range: (−∞,1]; 1 is perfect fit. 12 7.3 NLP Evaluation Metrics •BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy):Measures n-gram overlap between generated and reference text. Used for machine translation. •ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation):Measures recall of n-grams between generated and reference summaries. •Perplexity:Measures how well a language model predicts a text sample. Lower is better. Defined as 2 H(p,q) whereHis cross-entropy.
concepts: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve

text: recall of n-grams between generated and reference summaries. •Perplexity:Measures how well a language model predicts a text sample. Lower is better. Defined as 2 H(p,q) whereHis cross-entropy. •BERTScore:Uses contextual embeddings from BERT to compute semantic sim- ilarity between generated and reference text. 8 Data Engineering for Machine Learning 8.1 Feature Engineering Feature Engineeringis the process of using domain knowledge to transform raw data into features that improve model performance. It is often considered the most impactful step in the ML pipeline. Techniques include: •One-Hot Encoding:Converting categorical variables into binary vectors. •Normalization:Scaling features to a range (e.g., [0,1]). •Standardization:Transforming features to zero mean and unit variance. •Polynomial Features:Creating interaction terms from existing features. •Log Transformation:Reducing skewness in distributions. 8.2 Data Splitting A standard ML workflow splits data into three sets:

•Polynomial Features:Creating interaction terms from existing features. •Log Transformation:Reducing skewness in distributions. 8.2 Data Splitting A standard ML workflow splits data into three sets: •Training Set:Used to fit the model’s parameters. Typically 60–80% of data. •Validation Set:Used to tune hyperparameters and prevent overfitting. Typically 10–20%. •Test Set:Used only once to evaluate the final model’s performance. Typically 10–20%. Data leakageoccurs when information from the test set or future data accidentally influences the training process, resulting in inflated evaluation metrics. 8.3 Handling Class Imbalance Class imbalanceoccurs when one class has significantly fewer samples than another (e.g., fraud detection where fraud cases are rare). Techniques to address this: 13 •Oversampling:Increasing minority class samples (e.g., SMOTE — Synthetic Mi- nority Over-sampling Technique). •Undersampling:Reducing majority class samples.

13 •Oversampling:Increasing minority class samples (e.g., SMOTE — Synthetic Mi- nority Over-sampling Technique). •Undersampling:Reducing majority class samples. •Class Weights:Assigning higher loss weights to minority class examples. •Threshold Adjustment:Modifying the decision threshold for the positive class. 8.4 Dimensionality Reduction Dimensionality Reductionrefers to techniques that reduce the number of features while preserving as much information as possible. •Principal Component Analysis (PCA):A linear technique that projects data onto orthogonal components that explain maximum variance. •t-SNE (t-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding):A non-linear tech- nique for visualization, preserving local structure in 2D/3D. •UMAP (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection):Faster than t-SNE; preserves both local and global structure. •Autoencoders:Neural networks trained to encode inputs into a lower-dimensional latent space and decode them back. 9 Ethics and Responsible AI

t-SNE; preserves both local and global structure. •Autoencoders:Neural networks trained to encode inputs into a lower-dimensional latent space and decode them back. 9 Ethics and Responsible AI 9.1 AI Bias AI Biasrefers to systematic errors in AI outputs that create unfair outcomes for certain groups. Bias can enter AI systems at multiple stages: •Historical Bias:The training data reflects existing societal inequalities. •Representation Bias:Certain groups are underrepresented in training data. •Measurement Bias:Inaccurate data collection disproportionately affects sub- groups. •Aggregation Bias:A single model is applied to groups with meaningfully different characteristics. 9.2 Fairness Definitions Several mathematical definitions of fairness have been proposed: •Demographic Parity:The model’s positive prediction rate is equal across groups. •Equalized Odds:Both true positive rate and false positive rate are equal across groups.

•Demographic Parity:The model’s positive prediction rate is equal across groups. •Equalized Odds:Both true positive rate and false positive rate are equal across groups. •Individual Fairness:Similar individuals receive similar predictions. 14 It has been mathematically proven that certain fairness criteria are mutually incompatible when base rates differ across groups (impossibility results). 9.3 Explainability and Interpretability •Interpretability:The degree to which a human can understand the cause of a model’s decision. •Explainability:The ability to provide a post-hoc explanation of a model’s behav- ior. Key tools: •LIME (Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations):Approximates a complex model locally with a simpler interpretable one. •SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations):Assigns each feature a contribution score based on Shapley values from cooperative game theory. 9.4 Constitutional AI Constitutional AI (CAI)is a framework developed by Anthropic for training AI sys-

score based on Shapley values from cooperative game theory. 9.4 Constitutional AI Constitutional AI (CAI)is a framework developed by Anthropic for training AI sys- tems to be helpful, harmless, and honest. It uses a set of explicit principles (a “con- stitution”) and self-critique to iteratively refine model outputs without relying solely on human feedback for every revision. 10 Optimization and Training Techniques 10.1 Gradient Descent Gradient Descentis an iterative optimization algorithm that minimizes a loss function by updating parameters in the direction of the negative gradient: θ←θ−η∇ θL(θ) whereηis thelearning rateandLis the loss function. Variants: •Batch Gradient Descent:Computes gradient over the entire dataset. Slow for large datasets. •Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD):Computes gradient on a single sample. Noisy but fast. •Mini-Batch Gradient Descent:Computes gradient on a small batch. Most common in practice. 10.2 Adaptive Optimizers

Noisy but fast. •Mini-Batch Gradient Descent:Computes gradient on a small batch. Most common in practice. 10.2 Adaptive Optimizers •Momentum:Accumulates a velocity vector in directions of persistent gradient to accelerate convergence. 15 •AdaGrad:Adapts learning rates per parameter based on historical gradient mag- nitudes. •RMSProp:Uses a moving average of squared gradients to normalize the gradient. •Adam (Adaptive Moment Estimation):Combines momentum and RMSProp. Most widely used optimizer in deep learning. Proposed by Kingma and Ba (2014). •AdamW:Adam with decoupled weight decay. Preferred for training transformers. 10.3 Regularization Techniques •L1 Regularization (Lasso):Addsλ P |wi|to the loss. Encourages sparsity. •L2 Regularization (Ridge):Addsλ P w2 i to the loss. Prevents large weights. •Elastic Net:Combines L1 and L2 penalties. •Dropout:Randomly deactivates a fraction of neurons during training to prevent co-adaptation.

i to the loss. Prevents large weights. •Elastic Net:Combines L1 and L2 penalties. •Dropout:Randomly deactivates a fraction of neurons during training to prevent co-adaptation. •Batch Normalization:Normalizes activations within a mini-batch, stabilizing training and acting as implicit regularization. 10.4 Learning Rate Scheduling Learning Rate Schedulingadjusts the learning rate during training to improve con- vergence: •Step Decay:Reduce LR by a factor everynepochs. •Cosine Annealing:LR follows a cosine curve, periodically restarting. •Warmup:Gradually increase LR from near zero at the start of training. •ReduceLROnPlateau:Reduce LR when validation loss stops improving. 11 Emerging Paradigms 11.1 Transfer Learning Transfer Learningis a technique where a model trained on one task is reused as a start- ing point for a different but related task. It leverages previously learned representations to reduce training data requirements and computation.

ing point for a different but related task. It leverages previously learned representations to reduce training data requirements and computation. Fine-tuningrefers to continuing training a pre-trained model on a new, smaller dataset, adjusting some or all of the model’s weights. 11.2 Few-Shot and Zero-Shot Learning •Zero-Shot Learning:The model performs a task without any task-specific train- ing examples, relying on prior knowledge. 16 •Few-Shot Learning:The model performs a task given only a small number of labeled examples (e.g., 1-shot, 5-shot). •In-Context Learning:LLMs can perform few-shot learning by including exam- ples in the prompt without any gradient updates. 11.3 Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) PEFTmethods allow fine-tuning of large models while updating only a small subset of parameters: •LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation):Injects trainable low-rank matrices into exist- ing weight matrices. Introduced by Hu et al. (2021). Widely used for efficient LLM fine-tuning.

parameters: •LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation):Injects trainable low-rank matrices into exist- ing weight matrices. Introduced by Hu et al. (2021). Widely used for efficient LLM fine-tuning. •Prefix Tuning:Prepends trainable tokens to the input of each transformer layer. •Prompt Tuning:Optimizes soft prompt tokens while keeping the model frozen. •Adapter Layers:Small bottleneck layers inserted between transformer layers. 11.4 Diffusion Models Diffusion Modelsare a class of generative models that learn to reverse a gradual noising process. During training, data is progressively corrupted with Gaussian noise; the model learns to denoise. During inference, the model generates new data by iteratively denoising random noise. Examples:DDPM(Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models),Stable Diffusion,DALL- E 2,Imagen. 11.5 Multimodal Models Multimodal modelsprocess and generate multiple types of data (e.g., text, images, audio). Key examples:

E 2,Imagen. 11.5 Multimodal Models Multimodal modelsprocess and generate multiple types of data (e.g., text, images, audio). Key examples: •CLIP(OpenAI): Jointly trains image and text encoders using contrastive learning on image-caption pairs. •GPT-4V:GPT-4 with vision capabilities. •LLaVA:Open-source visual instruction-tuned model. •Gemini 1.5 Pro:Processes text, images, audio, and video with a 1M token context window. 11.6 Mixture of Experts (MoE) Mixture of Experts (MoE)is an architecture where the model consists of multiple “expert” sub-networks, and agating networkroutes each input token to a small subset of experts. This allows very large model capacity with sub-linear compute per token. Notable MoE models includeMixtral 8x7B(Mistral AI) andGPT-4(reported to use MoE architecture). 17 12 Summary Table of Key Terms TermDefinition OverfittingModel performs well on training data but poorly on unseen data UnderfittingModel is too simple to capture the data’s underly- ing pattern
